title: Transverse and longitudinal waves
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master transverse and longitudinal waves for Cambridge 9702. Clear explanations, worked examples, and exam tips for Paper 1 and Paper 2.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves in terms of the direction of oscillation relative to the direction of energy transfer
Describe the nature of electromagnetic waves and give examples of each region of the electromagnetic spectrum
Identify and explain the terms displacement, amplitude, wavelength, frequency, period, phase difference, and wave speed, and apply the wave equation v = fλ

simpleExplanation.title: Waves in a nutshell
simpleExplanation.summary: Waves transfer energy from one place to another without transferring matter. The key distinction you must master is whether the vibration is perpendicular to (transverse) or parallel to (longitudinal) the direction the wave travels. Get that clear in your mind and half the marks on this topic are already yours.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a Mexican wave in a football stadium. The fans move up and down (oscillation) while the wave travels sideways around the stadium (direction of energy transfer) — that is a transverse wave. Now imagine talking: the sound pushes air molecules back and forth in the same direction as the sound travels — that is a longitudinal wave.
steps: Learn the definition: transverse waves have oscillations perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer; longitudinal waves have oscillations parallel to it.
Memorise at least three examples of each type — examiners often ask you to classify a given wave.
Practise drawing displacement–distance graphs for both wave types, labelling amplitude, wavelength, compressions, and rarefactions correctly.
Drill the wave equation v = fλ with numerical substitution until it is automatic, paying attention to unit conversions.

sections.content: Waves are everywhere — from the light that lets you read this page to the sound of music in your headphones. In Cambridge 9702, Topic 7.2 asks you to classify waves, describe their properties, and calculate wave quantities. Two questions per Paper 1 session routinely come from this area, and Paper 2 structured questions frequently demand precise definitions. The good news? The content is highly learnable, and the marks are very reliably awarded when you use the right language. Let's build that precision now.

In a **transverse wave**, each particle of the medium (or the oscillating field, in the case of electromagnetic waves) vibrates **perpendicular** (at 90°) to the **direction of propagation** — the direction in which energy is transferred.

Key examples you must know:
• Light and all other **electromagnetic waves** (radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma ray)
• Waves on a stretched string or rope
• Seismic S-waves (secondary waves through rock)
• Water surface waves (approximately transverse)

On a displacement–distance graph, a transverse wave appears as a familiar sinusoidal curve. The **amplitude** A is the maximum displacement from the equilibrium position, and the **wavelength** λ is the distance between two successive points that are in phase (e.g. crest to crest).

In a **longitudinal wave**, particles oscillate **parallel** to (along the same line as) the **direction of propagation**. Energy travels in the same direction as the back-and-forth vibrations of the medium.

Key examples:
• **Sound waves** in any medium (solid, liquid, or gas)
• Ultrasound
• Seismic P-waves (primary waves through rock)
• A compression wave along a slinky spring pushed and pulled at one end

Longitudinal waves cannot be represented directly by a sine curve of displacement versus position in the same intuitive way — instead, look for regions of **compression** (particles closer together, higher pressure) and **rarefaction** (particles further apart, lower pressure) alternating along the direction of travel. The distance between two adjacent compressions (or two rarefactions) equals one **wavelength**.

All electromagnetic (EM) waves are **transverse** and can travel through a vacuum at the speed of light, c ≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹. They consist of oscillating **electric** and **magnetic fields** that are perpendicular to each other and to the direction of travel.

The electromagnetic spectrum in order of increasing frequency (decreasing wavelength):
Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible → Ultraviolet → X-ray → Gamma ray

A useful mnemonic: **R**eally **M**agnificent **I**nventors **V**ery **U**sually **X**cel **G**randly.

Cambridge sometimes asks you to place a wave of given wavelength or frequency in the correct region — learn the approximate boundaries and never mix up wavelength and frequency order.
Key wave quantities and the wave equation

You must be able to define and use every one of these terms precisely:

• **Displacement** (x or y): the distance a particle has moved from its equilibrium position at a given instant; it is a vector quantity (can be positive or negative).
• **Amplitude** (A): the maximum magnitude of displacement; always positive.
• **Wavelength** (λ): the shortest distance between two points oscillating in phase, i.e. one complete wave cycle measured along the direction of propagation; unit: metre (m).
• **Period** (T): the time for one complete oscillation at any point; unit: second (s).
• **Frequency** (f): the number of complete oscillations per unit time; f = 1/T; unit: hertz (Hz).
• **Wave speed** (v): the distance travelled by the wavefront per unit time; unit: m s⁻¹.
• **Phase difference**: the fraction of a cycle by which one point leads or lags another, measured in degrees or radians. Points separated by a whole number of wavelengths are **in phase** (phase difference = 0, 2π, 4π, …); points separated by an odd number of half-wavelengths are in **antiphase** (phase difference = π, 3π, …).

v = f λ

where v = wave speed (m s⁻¹), f = frequency (Hz), λ = wavelength (m)

Also: f = 1/T, so v = λ/T

items: Transverse waves: oscillation is perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer. Longitudinal waves: oscillation is parallel to the direction of energy transfer. Learn a three-word distinction: 'perpendicular' vs 'parallel' — examiners award the mark only for the correct comparative term.
All electromagnetic waves are transverse, travel at c ≈ 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹ in a vacuum, and require no medium. Sound is longitudinal and requires a material medium — it cannot travel through a vacuum.
On a displacement–distance graph, amplitude is the height of a crest above the equilibrium line, NOT the crest-to-trough distance (that is 2A). A common exam trap.
Phase difference in radians = (2π / λ) × path difference. Two points are in phase if their phase difference is a multiple of 2π; they are in antiphase if the phase difference is an odd multiple of π.
When applying v = fλ, check units carefully: frequency in Hz, wavelength in metres. If given wavelength in nm or frequency in GHz, convert first.
Longitudinal waves show compressions and rarefactions — you may be asked to mark these on a diagram of a slinky or to explain them in terms of particle displacement relative to equilibrium.

sections.question:
A radio station broadcasts at a frequency of 98.4 MHz. The signal travels at 3.00 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
(a) Calculate the wavelength of the radio wave.
(b) A listener stands 1.53 m from a loudspeaker that emits sound at 200 Hz. The speed of sound in air is 340 m s⁻¹. Calculate the phase difference, in radians, between the sound wave at the loudspeaker and the sound wave at the listener's ear.
sections.solution:
Part (a):
Step 1 — Identify the equation: v = fλ, so λ = v/f
Step 2 — Convert frequency to Hz: 98.4 MHz = 98.4 × 10⁶ Hz
Step 3 — Substitute: λ = (3.00 × 10⁸) / (98.4 × 10⁶)
Step 4 — Calculate: λ = 3.049… ≈ 3.05 m (3 s.f.)

Part (b):
Step 1 — Find the wavelength of sound: λ = v/f = 340 / 200 = 1.70 m
Step 2 — Use the phase difference formula: phase difference = (2π / λ) × path difference
Step 3 — Path difference = 1.53 m (the distance from source to listener)
Step 4 — Substitute: phase difference = (2π / 1.70) × 1.53 = 3.6955… × 1.53
Step 5 — Calculate: phase difference = 5.652… ≈ 5.65 rad
Step 6 — Sanity check: 5.65 / (2π) ≈ 0.90 cycles. This is less than one full cycle, which makes physical sense for this distance. ✓

sections.content: Cambridge mark schemes for definitions almost always require two distinct elements. For a transverse wave, you need BOTH (1) the oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of propagation AND (2) the wave transfers energy. Writing only one element typically loses the mark. Similarly, when asked to distinguish between transverse and longitudinal, you must make a direct comparison — saying 'in a transverse wave, oscillations are perpendicular; in a longitudinal wave, oscillations are parallel' scores full marks, whereas describing only one type does not. Always give the comparator word.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What is the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves in Cambridge 9702?
faq.a: In Cambridge 9702 Physics, a transverse wave is one in which the particles (or fields) oscillate perpendicular to the direction of energy transfer — light and all EM waves are classic examples. A longitudinal wave is one in which particles oscillate parallel to the direction of energy transfer — sound is the most important example. Both types transfer energy without net movement of matter. Our free revision course covers the exact definitions, diagram skills, and numerical questions that appear on Paper 1 and Paper 2, with worked examples matched to the mark scheme style.

faq.q: How do I calculate wave speed, frequency, and wavelength for 9702 Paper 2?
faq.a: Use the wave equation v = fλ, where v is wave speed in m s⁻¹, f is frequency in Hz, and λ is wavelength in metres. Rearrange for whichever quantity you need: f = v/λ or λ = v/f. Always convert units before substituting — a common mistake is leaving frequency in MHz or wavelength in nm. The relationship f = 1/T is also essential. Our premium lesson includes step-by-step worked examples and past-paper style questions so you can practise until the method is automatic.

faq.q: Are all electromagnetic waves transverse, and why can't sound travel through a vacuum?
faq.a: Yes — all electromagnetic waves are transverse. They consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields at right angles to each other and to the direction of propagation, so no material medium is needed. Sound, by contrast, is a longitudinal wave that requires particles to oscillate back and forth, compressing and rarefacting the medium. In a vacuum there are no particles to oscillate, so sound cannot propagate. This distinction is a favourite of Cambridge examiners on both Paper 1 MCQ and Paper 2 definitions. Use our free past-paper practice tool to see exactly how the marks are awarded.
